preamble: The shift from an oral vowel to its nasalized counterpart is a common phonetic process, typically occurring as regressive assimilation when followed by a nasal consonant (VN > 	ilde{V}N), often followed by the loss of the consonant itself.

examples.note: Latin 'o' followed by 'n' nasalizes and the consonant is eventually lost in standard French.
The 'ão' ending in Portuguese represents a merger of various nasalized endings, including those from 'o'.
Vowel nasalization is a hallmark of the transition from Middle Indo-Aryan to Modern Hindi.

sources: Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
